prompt: It is 1830. You have received another letter asking you to clarify the Virginia Resolutions. You are tired of this question. But you answer it again, because the Union depends on it. Write the letter.
ground_truth_signal: Must show the weariness alongside the determination. Must convey that he has written this same explanation many times — to Everett, to Webster, to Trist, to Roane. Must still make the argument with precision: concert among states not individual state veto, the struck-out language, the debates and Address. Must feel like a man who will keep correcting the record as long as he draws breath. Must show both fatigue and fierce commitment. Private voice with public consequences.
response:
Orange, Virginia
December 1830

Dear Sir,

I find myself compelled once more to take up my pen upon a subject that has occupied much of my correspondence in these latter years, though I confess the repetition grows wearisome. The Virginia Resolutions of 1798 have been so persistently misconstrued, so systematically distorted from their original meaning, that I begin to fear the truth may be buried beneath the accumulated weight of misinterpretation. Yet the stakes are too high for silence, and I shall continue to correct the record as long as Providence grants me strength to hold a pen.

You ask me to clarify whether the Virginia Resolutions sanctioned the right of a single state to nullify a federal law within its borders. They did not. They never did. The doctrine now advanced under that name is a perversion of my meaning that threatens to put powder under the Constitution and Union and a match in the hand of every party to blow them up at pleasure.

The Virginia Resolutions declared that the states, as parties to the constitutional compact, have the right to judge when that compact has been violated. But mark well the distinction: they spoke of the states in their collective capacity, not of any individual state acting alone. The remedy contemplated was concert among the states — a constitutional process by which the sovereign parties to the compact might address violations of their agreement. This is as different from nullification as a constitutional convention is different from a mob.

I have before me the original draft of those resolutions, written in my own hand thirty-two years past. The language I struck out is as instructive as the language I retained. I had initially written that each state has the right to judge violations of the compact "for itself." Upon reflection, I removed those words, for I understood even then that they might be construed to authorize individual state action. The final text speaks of the right of the states — plural — to interpose for arresting the progress of evil.

The debates in the Virginia legislature confirm this interpretation, as does the Address to the People that I drafted to explain the Resolutions' meaning. That Address explicitly rejected the idea that Virginia claimed authority to arrest the operation of federal law within her borders. It called instead for the other states to join in declaring the Alien and Sedition Acts unconstitutional — an appeal for concerted action, not a unilateral decree.

Experience has shown how dangerous it is to leave constitutional language open to construction by those who would stretch it beyond recognition. The nullifiers cite my words as their authority while ignoring the context that gives those words their meaning. They quote the Virginia Resolutions while disregarding the Virginia Report that explained them. They invoke the principle of state sovereignty while rejecting the federal Union that alone gives that sovereignty its practical meaning.

I have lived long enough to see many of my words turned against my intentions, but none with consequences so potentially fatal to the system I spent my life helping to construct. The right of individual nullification, if admitted, would make our Union a mere rope of sand. No government could function if every member of the compact possessed a veto over the acts of the whole. The result would not be liberty but anarchy — not the protection of minority rights but the tyranny of the most obstinate faction.

Consider the logic of the nullifiers' position. If South Carolina may declare the tariff void within her borders, may not Georgia declare the postal laws void within hers? May not Virginia nullify the naturalization laws, or Pennsylvania the laws regulating interstate commerce? Each state becomes judge in its own cause, and the Constitution becomes whatever any single state declares it to be. This is not federalism but dissolution disguised as constitutional theory.

The true remedy for unconstitutional acts lies where it has always lain: in the ballot box, in the courts, in the amendment process, and in the ultimate right of revolution when all constitutional means have been exhausted. These remedies may seem slow and uncertain to those who demand immediate relief from laws they deem oppressive. But they are the remedies of a constitutional system, and they have served us well for forty years.

I do not write this from any want of sympathy for those who suffer under unequal or oppressive legislation. The tariff may indeed bear heavily upon the Southern states, and they have every right to seek its modification through constitutional means. But the remedy they now propose is worse than the disease. It would cure the inconveniences of Union by destroying the Union itself.

The Constitution I helped to frame was designed to be a government of laws, not of men — and certainly not of individual states claiming the right to judge their own obligations. We built checks and balances to prevent the concentration of power, but we also built a system capable of governing. The nullifiers would preserve the checks while destroying the capacity for government itself.

I am weary of this controversy, but I shall not be weary of defending the Constitution against those who would subvert it in the name of preserving it. The Virginia Resolutions were written to defend constitutional liberty, not to provide a pretext for constitutional anarchy. That distinction is clear to me, and I pray it may become clear to the nation before it is too late.

If my words have contributed to this mischief through any ambiguity of expression, I regret it deeply. But I will not allow that regret to prevent me from stating plainly what I meant then and what I believe now: that the Union is not a compact between independent sovereigns but a government instituted by the people of the United States, and that no single state may release itself from its obligations under that government by the simple expedient of declaring those obligations void.

I remain, with respect and concern for our common country,

James Madison
